query: javascript block scope vs function scope var let const interview question

competitors.label: MDN let
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the strongest canonical reference for block scope, TDZ behavior, and loop binding semantics.
theirStrengths: It documents block scope, temporal dead zone behavior, and loop-binding semantics accurately.
theirGaps: It is keyword-reference material rather than a compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: MDN already covers loop bindings and scope visibility clearly enough that the practical difference is close.
actionableExamples: Its loop and block examples are concrete enough to keep the example criterion close.
followUpConfusion: MDN goes deeper on TDZ and declaration semantics, so it resolves more long-tail follow-up questions than our short trivia.

realWorldUseCases: Add one explicit switch-case or module-vs-script scope bug so the production angle is stronger than a keyword reference.
actionableExamples: Add one compact before/after loop example that contrasts shared `var` binding with per-iteration `let` binding.
followUpConfusion: Add one short note explaining how TDZ and block scope interact in interview follow-up questions.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd let / var / const
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep answer for the same var/let/const comparison.
theirStrengths: It is directly aligned with the interview phrasing and likely includes a clean side-by-side comparison.
theirGaps: It is less obviously focused on switch-case pitfalls and module-vs-script behavior.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd targets the same interview framing, so the practical use-case criterion stays close.
actionableExamples: Its side-by-side comparison format is likely stronger than our current shorter worked examples.
followUpConfusion: Our page mentions the shared-loop-binding trap, but the overall follow-up coverage is still close rather than clearly superior.

realWorldUseCases: Make one switch-case or global-scope example more explicit so our page surfaces a bug GreatFrontEnd is less likely to foreground.
actionableExamples: Add one tiny table or code comparison that puts `var`, `let`, and `const` behavior side by side.
followUpConfusion: Add one direct answer to the inevitable 'So is JavaScript block scoped or function scoped?' follow-up.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks let
competitors.selectionReason: This beginner-focused tutorial is a common reference competitor for `let` semantics and block scoping basics.
competitors.winReason: Our page is stronger on the interview trap itself and on why JavaScript presents both function and block scoping depending on the declaration keyword.
theirStrengths: It explains `let`, block scope, and redeclaration rules clearly for beginners.
theirGaps: It is not structured around the bigger comparison between function-scoped `var` and block-scoped `let`/`const`.

realWorldUseCases: Strong answers explain loop behavior, closure bugs, switch-case pitfalls, and module-vs-script global scope differences.
followUpConfusion: So the right interview answer is not one word. It is: 'Both, depending on declaration keyword.'

realWorldUseCases: GeeksforGeeks focuses on `let` semantics, but it does not connect the topic as strongly to loop-capture bugs and interview traps.
actionableExamples: It includes straightforward scope examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: It explains `let` itself, but not the larger follow-up of why JavaScript can be both function-scoped and block-scoped.

actionableExamples: Add one more compact example that contrasts block, function, and module scope in one place.
